1: "I will praise you, LORD, with all my heart; before the gods (nĕgeḏ ʾĕlōhîm) I will sing your praise" — "before the gods" is one of the most discussed phrases in this psalm. Options: (1) the divine council / heavenly court (as in Ps 82:1; 89:7; Job 1-2) — YHWH is praised in the presence of the heavenly assembly; (2) before human judges and rulers (as in Exod 21:6; 22:8-9 where ʾĕlōhîm = judges); (3) at the temple where images of foreign gods might be present (less likely). The divine council reading fits best: the psalmist praises YHWH in the presence of the heavenly beings, asserting YHWH's superiority. This connects to v. 2 (YHWH's name above all) and v. 5 (the kings will sing when they hear YHWH's words).
2: "I will bow down toward your holy temple and will praise your name for your unfailing love and your faithfulness, for you have so exalted your solemn decree (ʾimrātĕkā) that it surpasses your fame (šimkā)" — a notoriously difficult verse. "You have exalted your word above all your name" — most natural reading: the divine word/promise has been fulfilled in a way that even exceeds YHWH's already great reputation. The fulfillment of the ʾimrāh (word, decree, promise) validates and magnifies the šēm (name, reputation). The word is the active expression of the name — when YHWH's spoken word is fulfilled, his name is glorified beyond what it was before the fulfillment.
3: "When I called, you answered me; you greatly emboldened me (tarḥibēnî bĕnapšî ʿōz)" — "you emboldened me" — literally "you enlarged my soul with strength/boldness." The spatial metaphor: rāḥab (enlarge, make wide) is the opposite of the narrow, constricted soul in distress (Ps 4:1: "give me relief from my distress; have mercy on me and hear my prayer"). YHWH's answer to prayer creates interior expansion — a courage and boldness that came from the experience of being heard and answered. This transformation through answered prayer is the experiential confirmation of the divine attributes celebrated in v. 2 (love and faithfulness).
4: "May all the kings of the earth praise you, LORD, when they hear what you have decreed" — the universal dimension: the divine word that emboldened the individual psalmist (v. 3) is also what will cause all kings of the earth to praise YHWH when they hear it. "What you have decreed (ʾimrê-pîkā)" — the words of your mouth, the divine oracles. The Zion pilgrimage eschatology again: kings come to Zion to hear YHWH's words (cf. Ps 68:29, 32; 72:10-11; Isa 2:2-4). The progression from individual prayer answered (v. 3) to universal royal praise (v. 4) is the psalm's trajectory: covenant experience of the one becomes the testimony that draws the many.
5: "May they sing of the ways of the LORD, for the glory of the LORD is great" — "the ways of the LORD (darkê YHWH)" — the patterns of divine action in history, the moral logic of YHWH's governance. Knowing YHWH's "ways" is deeper covenant knowledge than knowing his "acts" (Ps 103:7: "He made known his ways to Moses, his deeds to the people of Israel" — Moses knew the deeper pattern; the people saw only specific acts). The kings are invited to sing not just about specific divine acts but about the overarching pattern of YHWH's governance — the ḥeseḏ and ʾĕmet of v. 2 as the characteristic mode of divine engagement with history.
6: "Though the LORD is exalted, he looks kindly on the lowly (šāpāl); though lofty, he sees the arrogant (gābōah) from afar" — the fundamental reversal of normal social logic. In ANE royal ideology, the king on his throne sees only those presented to him — the powerful, the wealthy, the well-connected. YHWH, though supremely exalted (rām), attends specifically to the lowly (šāpāl — brought low, humbled). Mary's Magnificat (Luke 1:51-52) draws directly on this verse: "He has brought down rulers from their thrones but has lifted up the humble." The arrogant he "sees from afar" — not with the intimacy of personal engagement but with the cold distance of a sovereign who acknowledges their existence but grants them no access.
7: "Though I walk in the midst of trouble, you preserve my life; you stretch out your hand against the wrath of my foes; your right hand will save me" — the confidence based on the theological principle of v. 6. Because YHWH attends to the lowly, the psalmist can walk "in the midst of trouble (bĕqereb ṣārāh)" with confidence. "You preserve my life (tĕḥayyênî)" — the causative form of "to live" — YHWH actively keeps the psalmist alive. "Stretch out your hand against the wrath of my foes" — the same divine arm that reached into Egypt to rescue Israel (Exod 6:6 — "outstretched arm") reaches into personal crisis to rescue the individual. The cosmic rescue pattern applies at personal scale.
8: "The LORD will vindicate me (yigmōr baʿăḏî YHWH); your love, LORD, endures forever — do not abandon the works of your hands" — "vindicates me (gāmar)" — will complete or perfect on my behalf; will accomplish what he has started for me. The theological grounding: YHWH's "love (ḥeseḏ) endures forever" — the same ḥeseḏ refrain as Ps 136, here applied to the individual. "Works of your hands (maʿăśēh yāḏêkā)" — a creation image: as YHWH does not abandon what he has made in creation, he does not abandon what he has made in covenant relationship. The psalmist is YHWH's workmanship (Eph 2:10 uses the same logic: "we are God's handiwork") — the creator's investment ensures completion.
